analogy: Think of "Change and Development in Rural Society" through the chapter's own opening images: Social Change and Development in India 42 Indian society is primarily a rural society though urbanisation is growing. The majority of India’s people live in rural areas (69 per cent, according to the 2011 Census). They make their living from agriculture or related occupations. This... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 2011 Census). They make their living from agriculture or related occupations., 4.1 AgrAriAn Structure: cASte And, 4.6 Globalisation, liberalisation,, Change and Development in Rural Society. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Change and Development in Rural Society is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that social Change and Development in India 42 Indian society is primarily a rural society though urbanisation is growing.

The text explains that the majority of India’s people live in rural areas (69 per cent, according to the 2011 Census).

The text explains that they make their living from agriculture or related occupations.

The text explains that this means that agricultural land is the most important productive resource for a great many Indians.

The text explains that land is also the most important form of property.

The text explains that but land is not just a ‘means of production’ nor just a ‘form of property’.

The text explains that nor is agriculture just a form of livelihood.

The text explains that many of our cultural practices and patterns can be traced to our agrarian backgrounds.

The text explains that you will recall from the earlier chapters how closely interrelated structural and cultural changes are.

The text explains that for example, most of the New Year festivals in different regions of India – such as Pongal in Tamil Nadu, Bihu in Assam, Baisakhi in Punjab and Ugadi in Karnataka to name just a few – actually celebrate the main harvest season and herald the beginning of a new agricultural season.

The text explains that different means of agriculture and related festivals.

The text explains that there is a close connection between agriculture and culture.

The text explains that the nature and practice of agriculture varies greatly across the different regions of the country.

The text explains that these variations are reflected in the different regional cultures.

The text explains that one can say that both the culture and social structure in rural India are closely bound up with agricultural and the agrarian way of life.

The text explains that agriculture is the single most important source of livelihood for the majority of the rural population.

The text explains that many activities that support agriculture and village life are also sources of livelihood for people in rural India.

The text explains that they were once part and parcel of the village economy.

The text explains that you have already read in Chapter 1 how the influx of manufactured goods replaced hand-made products.

The text explains that change and Development in Rural Society 43 diversity of occupations in rural India was reflected in the caste system, which in most regions included specialist and ‘service’ castes such as Dry Cleaners, Potters and Goldsmiths.

The text explains that for instance, there are rural residents employed in government services such as the Postal and Education Departments, factory workers, or in the army, who earn their living through non- agricultural activities.

The text explains that the Diversity of Occupations 43 ¾ Think of an important festival that is celebrated in your region that has its roots in agrarian society.

The text explains that social Change and Development in India 44 4.1 AgrAriAn Structure: cASte And clASS in rurAl indiA Agricultural land is the single most important resource and form of property in rural society.

The text explains that in most regions of India, women are usually excluded from ownership of land, because of the prevailing patrilineal kinship system and mode of inheritance.

The text explains that in reality, they only have limited rights and some access to land — only as part of a household headed by a man.

The text explains that the term agrarian structure is often used to refer to the structure or distribution of landholding.

The text explains that because agricultural land is the most important productive resource in rural areas, access to land shapes the rural class structure.

The text explains that access to land largely determines what role one plays in the process of agricultural production.

The text explains that agrarian society, therefore, can be understood in terms of its class structure.

The text explains that but we must also remember the structure is itself through the caste system.

The text explains that for instance, in most areas the highest caste, the Brahmins, are not major landowners, and so they fall outside the agrarian structure although they are a part of rural society.

The text explains that change and Development in Rural Society 45 very important.

The text explains that in each region, the dominant caste is the most powerful group, economically and politically, and dominates local society.

The text explains that the rough correspondence between caste and class means that typically the upper and middle castes also had the best access to land and resources, and hence to power and privilege.

The text explains that this had important implications for the rural economy and society.

The text explains that in most regions of the country, a ‘proprietary caste’ group owns most of the resources and can command labour to work for them.

The text explains that 4.2 the impAct of lAnd reformS the ColoniAl Period There are historical reasons why each region of India came to be dominated by just one or two major groups.

The text explains that but it is important to realise that this agrarian structure has changed enormously over time, from the pre-colonial to the There is a direct correspondence between agricultural productivity and the agrarian structure.

The text explains that here the most unequal agrarian structures developed.

The text explains that the agrarian structure of these regions was characterised by a large proportion of landless labourers, who were often ‘bonded’ workers belonging to the lowest castes.

The text explains that box 4.1 ¾ Think about what you have learned about the caste system.

The text explains that outline the various linkages between the agrarian or rural class structure and caste.

The text explains that discuss in terms of different access to resources, labour and occupation.

The text explains that while the same dominant castes were probably also cultivating castes in the pre-colonial period, they were not the direct owners of land.

The text explains that they also granted property rights to the zamindars.

The text explains that one result of this zamindari system was that agricultural production stagnated or declined during much of the period of British rule.

The text explains that many districts of colonial India were administered through the zamindari system.

The text explains that of Peasants, Migrants and Paupers; Rural labour Circulation and Capitalist Production in West India.

The text explains that 1999. ‘Geographical unevenness of India’s Green Revolution’, Journal of Contemporary Asia.

The text explains that postcolonial Developments: Agriculture in the Making of Modern India.

The text explains that farms and Factories; Social Profile of Large Farmers and Rural Industrialists in West India.

The text explains that 2006. ‘Political economy of agrarian distress’.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India, Chapter 4: Change and Development in Rural Society

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Change and Development in Rural Society, which statement is supported by the source text about Society?
options: In fact, the distribution of landholdings in most regions is highly unequal among households.
In some parts of India, the majority of rural households own at least some land – usually very small plots.
In other areas as much as 40 to 50 per cent of families do not own any land at all.
This means that they are dependent on agricultural labour or other kinds of work for their livelihoods.
